label: Uneasy calm
summary: The paper peace slipped further. Three days after reopening the Strait of Hormuz under the U.S.-Iran framework, Iran declared it closed again on Saturday, June 20, with the Revolutionary Guard warning ships away and the joint military command calling the move a 'first step' against what it framed as U.S. 'bad faith' and Israeli breaches. The trigger was Israel's continued strikes in southern Lebanon hours after the fresh Hezbollah ceasefire took hold, which killed more than a dozen people overnight. Washington disputed the closure: U.S. Central Command said passage actually increased, reporting 55 merchant vessels and roughly 17 million barrels of oil moving through the waterway on Saturday, and Vice President JD Vance said conditions had returned to pre-war levels even as he headed to Switzerland to try to salvage the talks. Markets stayed jittery rather than panicked, Brent hovering near $80 after an 8.5% weekly slide. Elsewhere the ledger was mixed. In the Americas, an Italian tourist died and some 1,690 guests were evacuated after fire gutted a beach resort in Bayahibe, Dominican Republic, while Colombia held a deeply polarized presidential runoff between far-right outsider Abelardo de la Espriella and leftist Ivan Cepeda. Ukraine and Russia traded fresh drone and missile strikes, with casualties in Kharkiv. Africa's Ebola Bundibugyo epidemic kept climbing, the WHO counting hundreds of confirmed cases and a roughly 20% fatality rate across DR Congo and Uganda. Against the gloom, science and health offered relief: a six-year-old in England regained full sight after gene therapy for a rare blinding disease, new data showed COVID vaccines sharply cut cardiovascular death, a perovskite catalyst turned waste heat into clean hydrogen, and conservationists hailed a global mangrove recovery. A ceasefire still nominally standing and oil still flowing keep the floor in place, but a renewed Hormuz standoff, dead in Lebanon and a worsening outbreak in central Africa pull the mood back below the neutral line.

drivers.headline: Iran declares the Strait of Hormuz closed again on June 20, just three days after reopening it, with the Revolutionary Guard warning ships away and the joint military command calling it a 'first step' against U.S. 'bad faith' and Israeli ceasefire breaches

Israeli forces keep striking southern Lebanon hours after the fresh Hezbollah ceasefire takes hold, killing more than a dozen people overnight and prompting Iran's closure of the strait over what it calls violations of the truce framework

Washington disputes the closure: U.S. Central Command says passage through the strait actually increased, with 55 merchant vessels and roughly 17 million barrels of oil transiting on Saturday, and VP JD Vance says conditions have returned to pre-war levels as he heads to Switzerland for talks
Oil stays jittery as the renewed Hormuz standoff casts doubt over the U.S.-Iran peace and nuclear talks, with Brent crude hovering near $80 a barrel after an 8.5% weekly slide

An Italian tourist is killed and some 1,690 guests evacuated, including 177 children and 21 babies, after a fast-moving fire fed by palm-roof structures guts the Viva Wyndham Dominicus Beach resort in Bayahibe, Dominican Republic

Colombia holds a deeply polarized presidential runoff between far-right outsider Abelardo de la Espriella, who led the first round with about 43.7%, and leftist Historic Pact candidate Ivan Cepeda, in a contest set to redefine ties with Washington

Ukraine and Russia trade fresh drone and missile strikes: a Russian guided-bomb attack on Kharkiv's Kholodnohirskyi district raises the death toll to nine, while Ukrainian forces hit energy and gas infrastructure in Crimea and southern regions

The WHO-declared Ebola Bundibugyo epidemic keeps climbing across DR Congo and Uganda, with hundreds of confirmed cases and a case-fatality rate around 20%, as the response is judged still insufficient one month in
A six-year-old girl in England regains full eyesight after pioneering gene therapy for Leber congenital amaurosis, a rare condition that had left her with severe low vision since infancy

New research finds COVID-19 vaccination lowered the risk of cardiovascular death from the virus by nearly 60%, heart attack by about 40% and stroke by over 30%, adding to evidence of broad protective benefits

A University of Birmingham team unveils a perovskite-based catalyst that splits water into hydrogen at far lower temperatures, letting factories, steel and cement plants and renewable sites turn waste heat into clean fuel

Conservationists report the world's mangrove forests have staged a remarkable global recovery, hailed as 'an important source of optimism for climate action,' alongside a striking rebound in South Asia's vultures

sources_consulted: Wikipedia (2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis / 2026 Israel-Lebanon ceasefire / 2026 Lebanon war / 2026 Colombian presidential election / 2026 Central Africa Ebola epidemic)
